title: Rinsing the Mouth and Nose
short_rule: Rinsing the mouth and nose is obligatory for Ghusl in the Hanafi and Hanbali schools, but is considered Sunnah in the Maliki school.
details: According to the Hanafi school, the obligatory integrals of the purificatory bath are washing the entire body, rinsing the mouth once, and rinsing the nose up to the bone once. The Hanbali school also categorizes rinsing the mouth and nostrils as mandatory acts of Ghusl. In contrast, the Maliki school classifies rinsing the mouth, inhaling water, and wetting the ear holes as Sunnahs of bathing, not absolute obligations.
actionable_steps: Rinse the nose up to the soft bone.
Wash the entire body, ensuring water reaches all parts.
quran_hadith_evidence: Derived from interpretations of complete bodily purification based on Prophetic practice.
spiritual_wisdom: Ensuring the complete purification of both the outer body and the facial openings prepares the believer physically and spiritually for standing before Allah.
source_book: Ascent to Felicity, al-Murshid al-Mu'in, Umdah al-Fiqh
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn 'Ashir, Ibn Qudamah

title: Rubbing (Dalk) and Continuity (Muwalat)
short_rule: The Maliki school uniquely mandates rubbing the body and washing continuously without a large break.
details: The Maliki school stipulates that the Wajibs (mandatory acts) of bathing include performing the parts of the bath one after the other without a long break (continuity), passing the hand or an instrument over the entire surface of the body (rubbing), and combing between the hair. If hard to reach, one must use a towel, rope, or appoint someone to rub. The Hanafi and Hanbali schools require that water simply encompasses the skin, without stipulating rubbing or strict continuity as obligatory integrals.
actionable_steps: Pass your hand, a towel, or a similar item over the entire surface of the body while washing.
Complete the washing of the body in a continuous sequence without taking a long break.
Ensure hidden parts like behind the knees, armpits, and buttocks are reached.
quran_hadith_evidence: Based on Maliki derivation of the linguistic and practical meaning of 'washing' (ghusl) in Arabic.
spiritual_wisdom: Rubbing and continuity prevent ritual washing from becoming a passive act, demanding physical mindfulness and ensuring absolute cleanliness.
author: Ibn 'Ashir

title: Intention for the Purificatory Bath
short_rule: The intention is mandatory in the Maliki and Hanbali schools, but is an emphasized Sunnah in the Hanafi school.
details: In both the Maliki and Hanbali schools, bringing the intention to mind is explicitly listed as the first mandatory act (Wajib) for Ghusl to be valid. In the Hanafi school, the intention is classified as an emphasized Sunnah rather than a Fard; meaning if a person simply immerses in water and washes the body, the ritual impurity is lifted, though the specific reward of the Sunnah is missed.
actionable_steps: Consciously form the intention to lift major ritual impurity before beginning the bath.
If following Maliki or Hanbali schools, ensure the intention is present as the bath begins.
quran_hadith_evidence: Deeds are but by their intentions. (Bukhari and Muslim)
spiritual_wisdom: The intention distinguishes an act of worship from a mere mundane habit of cooling off or cleaning dirt.
source_book: Ascent to Felicity, al-Murshid al-Mu'in, Umdah al-Fiqh
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn 'Ashir, Ibn Qudamah

title: Causes Necessitating Major Ablution
short_rule: Ghusl is required after ejaculation with pleasure, penetration, menstruation, or postnatal bleeding.
details: Across all presented schools (Hanafi, Maliki, Hanbali), major ritual impurity (Janabah) occurs, making Ghusl obligatory, under specific conditions: the emission of sperm/sexual fluid with pleasure (or gushing), and the disappearance of the head of the penis into a vagina or anus (penetration), even if ejaculation does not occur. Additionally, the cessation of menstruation (Hayd) and postnatal bleeding (Nifas) mandates Ghusl.
actionable_steps: Perform Ghusl after sexual intercourse, regardless of ejaculation.
Perform Ghusl after the emission of sexual fluid with pleasure (e.g., from a wet dream).
Perform Ghusl upon the completion of the menstrual or postnatal bleeding cycle.
quran_hadith_evidence: If you are in a state of Janabah (major ritual impurity), purify yourselves. (Quran 5:6)
spiritual_wisdom: Intense physical and emotional experiences like sexual climax or childbirth alter the spiritual state, requiring a complete washing to reset the body and soul for direct communion with Allah.
source_book: Ascent to Felicity, al-Murshid al-Mu'in, Umdah al-Fiqh
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn 'Ashir, Ibn Qudamah

title: Washing Braided Hair
short_rule: Women do not need to unbraid their hair for Ghusl following sexual impurity if the roots are fully saturated.
details: In both the Hanbali and Hanafi schools, a woman is not mandated to unbraid her hair for the purificatory bath after major sexual impurity (Janabah), provided that water thoroughly reaches and saturates the roots of the hair. However, for Ghusl following the end of menstruation, it is considered safer to unbraid the hair to ensure absolute purification.
actionable_steps: Ensure water completely saturates the roots of the hair.
Unbraiding is optional for sexual impurity, but recommended after menstruation.
quran_hadith_evidence: The Prophet instructed Umm Salamah: 'It is sufficient for you to pour three handfuls of water over your head and then pour water over yourself, and you will be purified.' (Muslim)
spiritual_wisdom: Islam balances the strict requirements of purity with the removal of undue hardship, recognizing the difficulty for women to constantly unbraid thick hair.
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn Qudamah
